related_cases: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
E.P. Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu
legal_explanation: Equality before the law under Article 14 ensures that no person is above the law and that all individuals are subject to the jurisdiction of ordinary courts, prohibiting arbitrary state discrimination.

related_cases: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar
Kathi Raning Rawat v. State of Saurashtra
legal_explanation: Equal protection of the laws guarantees equal treatment under equal circumstances. It permits reasonable classification by the state but prohibits class legislation.

related_cases: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
Joseph Shine v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 15 prohibits the state from discriminating against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth, or any of them, while allowing special provisions for women, children, and backward classes.

concept_name: Equality of Opportunity in Public Employment
related_cases: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
T. Devadasan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 16 guarantees equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State, permitting reservations for backward classes.

related_cases: State of Karnataka v. Appa Balu Ingale
People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 17 abolishes 'Untouchability' and forbids its practice in any form. The enforcement of any disability arising out of 'Untouchability' is an offense punishable in accordance with law.

related_cases: Balaji Raghavan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 18 prohibits the state from conferring any title (except military or academic distinctions) and prevents citizens from accepting titles from foreign states.

related_cases: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
Bennett Coleman v. Union of India
Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees all citizens the right to freedom of speech and expression, subject only to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) like public order, security of the state, and defamation.

related_cases: Babulal Parate v. State of Maharashtra
Ramlila Maidan Incident v. Home Secretary
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(b) guarantees the right to assemble peaceably and without arms, subject to reasonable restrictions in the interest of public order or the sovereignty and integrity of India.

related_cases: All India Bank Employees Association v. National Industrial Tribunal
O.K. Ghosh v. E.X. Joseph
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(c) guarantees the right to form associations, unions, or cooperative societies, subject to public order, morality, or sovereignty restrictions.

related_cases: Kharak Singh v. State of UP
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(d) guarantees the right to move freely throughout the territory of India, subject to restrictions in the interests of the general public or protection of Scheduled Tribes.

related_cases: State of UP v. Kaushaliya
Kharak Singh v. State of UP
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(e) guarantees the right to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India, subject to general public interest and tribal land protections.

concept_name: Freedom of Profession, Trade, and Business
related_cases: Chintaman Rao v. State of MP
State of Gujarat v. Mirzapur Moti Kureshi
T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
legal_explanation: Article 19(1)(g) guarantees the right to practice any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade, or business, subject to professional qualifications and state monopolies.

related_cases: Kedar Nath v. State of West Bengal
Soni Devrajbhai Babubhai v. State of Gujarat
legal_explanation: Article 20(1) protects individuals from being convicted of an offense under a law that was not in force at the time the act was committed, and from receiving a penalty greater than what was prescribed then.

related_cases: Maqbool Hussain v. State of Bombay
Thomas Dana v. State of Punjab
legal_explanation: Article 20(2) guarantees that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offense more than once.

related_cases: State of Bombay v. Kathi Kalu Oghad
Selvi v. State of Karnataka
legal_explanation: Article 20(3) provides that no person accused of any offense shall be compelled to be a witness against himself, protecting physical and mental autonomy.

concept_name: Right to Life and Personal Liberty
related_cases: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Francis Coralie Mullin v. Union of Territory of Delhi
legal_explanation: Article 21 guarantees that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law, which must be fair, just, and reasonable.

related_cases: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
Kharak Singh v. State of UP
Govind v. State of MP
PUCL v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Right to Privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21, governing spatial, informational, and decisional privacy.

related_cases: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corp.
DTC v. DTC Mazdoor Congress
legal_explanation: The right to livelihood is an essential facet of the right to life under Article 21, as depriving a person of livelihood would render life meaningless.

related_cases: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corp.
Chameli Singh v. State of UP
legal_explanation: The right to shelter is recognized as a fundamental right under Article 21, comprising adequate space, safe structure, clean surroundings, and security of tenure.

related_cases: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
Subhash Kumar v. State of Bihar
legal_explanation: Article 21 guarantees the right to live in a pollution-free environment, including clean air and water, establishing state duties to protect ecological balance.

related_cases: Parmanand Katara v. Union of India
Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal
legal_explanation: The right to health is fundamental under Article 21, obligating the state to provide medical assistance, especially in emergency situations, without procedural delays.

related_cases: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
Khatri v. State of Bihar
M.H. Hoskot v. State of Maharashtra
legal_explanation: Free legal aid is a fundamental right of an accused unable to afford legal counsel, stemming from Article 21 and Directive Principle Article 39A.

related_cases: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
Abdul Rehman Antulay v. R.S. Nayak
Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
legal_explanation: The right to a speedy and fair trial is an integral part of personal liberty under Article 21, prohibiting indefinite detention of undertrials.

related_cases: Unni Krishnan v. State of AP
Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka
T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
P.A. Inamdar v. State of Maharashtra
legal_explanation: Article 21A mandates that the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children aged six to fourteen years, implemented through the Right to Education Act.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
Joginder Kumar v. State of UP
Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
Lalita Kumari v. State of UP
Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
legal_explanation: Article 22 provides fundamental procedural safeguards against arbitrary arrest, including the right to be informed of grounds, consult a lawyer, and be produced before a magistrate within 24 hours.

related_cases: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Kharak Singh v. State of UP
Shaban Shafi v. State of J&K
legal_explanation: Preventive detention is detaining a person without trial to prevent them from committing future crimes. It is governed by strict safeguards and timelines under Article 22.

related_cases: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
Vishal Jeet v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 23(1) strictly prohibits traffic in human beings, begar, and other similar forms of forced labor, making any contravention an offense punishable by law.

related_cases: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
Sanjit Roy v. State of Rajasthan
legal_explanation: Begar or forced labor is prohibited under Article 23. Paying less than the minimum wage is judicially recognized as a form of forced labor violating Article 23.

related_cases: M.C. Mehta v. State of Tamil Nadu
People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 24 prohibits the employment of children below the age of fourteen years in any factory, mine, or other hazardous employment.

related_cases: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala
Sabarimala Case
Shayara Bano v. Union of India
Commissioner, Hindu Religious Endowments, Madras v. Sri Lakshmindra Thirtha Swamiar of Sri Shirur Mutt
legal_explanation: Articles 25-28 guarantee freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion, subject to public order, morality, and health.

related_cases: S.P. Mittal v. Union of India
Sabarimala Case
legal_explanation: Article 26 guarantees every religious denomination or section the right to establish and maintain institutions, manage their own affairs in religion, and own and administer property.

related_cases: Commissioner, Hindu Religious Endowments v. Lakshmindra
legal_explanation: Article 27 provides that no person shall be compelled to pay any taxes, the proceeds of which are specifically appropriated in payment of expenses for the promotion of any particular religion.

concept_name: Freedom from Religious Instruction in State Schools
related_cases: Aruna Roy v. Union of India
D.A.V. College v. State of Punjab
legal_explanation: Article 28 prohibits religious instruction in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds and forbids compulsory attendance at religious instruction in state-aided schools.

related_cases: State of Bombay v. Bombay Education Society
D.A.V. College v. State of Punjab
legal_explanation: Article 29 protects any section of citizens residing in India who have a distinct language, script, or culture, giving them the right to conserve the same.

related_cases: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
P.A. Inamdar v. State of Maharashtra
State of Kerala v. Very Rev. Mother Provincial
legal_explanation: Article 30 grants all minorities, whether based on religion or language, the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.

related_cases: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 32 provides the right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of fundamental rights, described by Dr. Ambedkar as the heart and soul of the Constitution.

related_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Jilubhai Nanbhai Khachar v. State of Gujarat
legal_explanation: Article 300A provides that no person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law, converting property from a fundamental right to a constitutional right.

related_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Judicial review is the power of the courts to examine the validity of legislative acts and executive decisions, declared as a part of the basic structure of the Constitution.

related_cases: E.P. Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Shayara Bano v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Arbitrariness states that equality and arbitrariness are sworn enemies. Any state action that is arbitrary, unreasonable, or lacking classification violates Article 14.

related_cases: Modern Dental College v. State of MP
Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Proportionality is a judicial standard requiring state actions restricting rights to have a legitimate aim, rational connection, necessity, and balanced impact.

related_cases: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
State of Orissa v. Binapani Dei
legal_explanation: Audi Alteram Partem is the principle of natural justice ensuring that no one is condemned unheard. It mandates prior notice and a fair opportunity to represent one's case.

concept_name: Natural Justice - Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
related_cases: A.K. Kraipak v. Union of India
Mineral Development Ltd. v. State of Bihar
legal_explanation: Nemo Judex in Causa Sua mandates that no one shall be a judge in their own cause, prohibiting pecuniary, personal, or policy bias in decision makers.

related_cases: Food Corporation of India v. Kamdhenu Cattle Feeds
Union of India v. International Trading Co.
legal_explanation: The doctrine of Legitimate Expectation protects individuals who expect a benefit or procedure to continue based on past state representations, unless overridden by public interest.

related_cases: Motilal Padampat Sugar Mills v. State of UP
Union of India v. Anglo Afghan Agencies
legal_explanation: Promissory Estoppel binds the state to fulfill promises made to citizens if the citizen has acted upon that promise and altered their position to their detriment.

related_cases: State of UP v. Raj Narain
ADR v. Union of India
PUCL v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Right to Information is a fundamental right implicit under freedom of speech and expression Article 19(1)(a), enabling citizens to hold the government accountable.

related_cases: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
Bennett Coleman v. Union of India
Express Newspapers v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Freedom of the press, although not explicitly mentioned in the Indian Constitution, is protected under the broader right to freedom of speech and expression.

related_cases: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
Faheema Shirin v. State of Kerala
legal_explanation: Access to the internet is a fundamental right nested under freedom of speech Article 19(1)(a) and personal liberty Article 21, and suspensions must satisfy proportionality tests.

related_cases: Lata Singh v. State of UP
Shafin Jahan v. Asokan K.M.
Supriyo v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The right to marry a person of one's choice is a fundamental component of personal liberty and dignity under Article 21.

related_cases: Air India v. Nergesh Meerza
Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
Secretary, Ministry of Defence v. Babita Puniya
legal_explanation: Gender equality is guaranteed through the interplay of Articles 14, 15, and 16, prohibiting discrimination based on sex and enabling affirmative action.

related_cases: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
Apparel Export Promotion Council v. A.K. Chopra
legal_explanation: Sexual harassment at the workplace is recognized as a direct violation of gender equality (Article 14) and the right to life with dignity (Article 21), leading to the Vishaka guidelines.

related_cases: State of MP v. Ram Kishna Balothia
M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Constitution safeguards Scheduled Castes through reservation, abolition of untouchability, and the establishment of the National Commission for Scheduled Castes under Article 338.

related_cases: Samatha v. State of Andhra Pradesh
State of Kerala v. Peoples Union for Democratic Liberties
legal_explanation: Scheduled Tribes are protected through cultural preservation, land alienation laws, reservations, and the National Commission for STs under Article 338A.

related_cases: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
Ashoka Kumar Thakur v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The state is empowered to create special provisions and job reservations for Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs) and OBCs under Articles 15(4) and 16(4).

related_cases: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta
legal_explanation: The Creamy Layer concept excludes affluent backward class individuals from reservations, ensuring benefits reach the genuinely marginalized.

related_cases: Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment introduced a 10% reservation in educational admissions and public employment for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) of citizens.

related_cases: Union of India v. National Federation of the Blind
Rajeev Kumar Gupta v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Disability rights ensure equal access, non-discrimination, and reasonable accommodation in public spaces and state employment under Articles 14 and 21.

related_cases: Suresh Kumar Koushal v. Naz Foundation
Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
Supriyo v. Union of India
legal_explanation: LGBTQ individuals are guaranteed rights of equal protection, privacy, dignity, and expression, leading to the decriminalization of consensual same-sex acts.

related_cases: National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Supreme Court recognized transgenders as 'third gender' and affirmed their right to self-identify, ensuring protections under equality and liberty articles.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
Selvi v. State of Karnataka
legal_explanation: Accused persons are protected against ex-post facto laws, double jeopardy, and self-incrimination, along with the right to legal aid and fair trial.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
State of Delhi v. Ravji
legal_explanation: Police custody involves detention by the police for investigation, which must be authorized by a magistrate and is subject to strict constitutional guidelines.

related_cases: Sanjay Chandra v. CBI
Dharampal v. State of Haryana
legal_explanation: Judicial custody refers to keeping an accused person in jail under the command of the magistrate, distinct from police custody, protecting against police coercion.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
Sheela Barse v. State of Maharashtra
Nilabati Behera v. State of Orissa
legal_explanation: Custodial violence, torture, and deaths violate the right to life and bodily integrity under Article 21, making the state liable to pay compensation.

related_cases: Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab
State of Rajasthan v. Balchand
Sanjay Chandra v. CBI
legal_explanation: Bail is the release of an accused person from custody. The guiding principle is 'bail is the rule, jail is the exception' to protect personal liberty.

related_cases: Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab
Sushila Aggarwal v. State of NCT Delhi
legal_explanation: Anticipatory bail is a pre-arrest direction by a court to release a person on bail if arrested, preventing harassment and arbitrary detention.

related_cases: Lalita Kumari v. State of UP
legal_explanation: Registration of an FIR is mandatory under Section 154 of the CrPC if the information discloses the commission of a cognizable offense.

related_cases: M.P. Sharma v. Satish Chandra
Kharak Singh v. State of UP
Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Search and seizure operations by law enforcement must be legally authorized and reasonable, balancing investigation needs with privacy.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
Tarapada De v. State of West Bengal
legal_explanation: Article 22(1) mandates that no person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest.

related_cases: State of Punjab v. Ajaib Singh
Manoj v. State of MP
legal_explanation: Every person arrested must be produced before the nearest magistrate within twenty-four hours (excluding travel time) and cannot be detained further without magistrate sanction.

related_cases: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
State of MP v. Shobharam
legal_explanation: Article 22(1) guarantees the right of an arrested person to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of their choice.

related_cases: Prem Shankar Shukla v. Delhi Administration
Citizens for Democracy v. State of Assam
legal_explanation: Handcuffing is an affront to human dignity. It is forbidden unless there is a clear, documented risk of escape by the prisoner.

related_cases: Sunil Batra v. Delhi Administration
Kishore Singh v. State of Rajasthan
legal_explanation: Solitary confinement is a harsh punishment violating Article 21, and can only be imposed in exceptional circumstances under strict judicial oversight.

related_cases: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Hajibhai v. State of Gujarat
legal_explanation: Article 22 governs preventive detention. The detainee must be given the earliest opportunity to make a representation against the detention order.

related_cases: Mohinder Singh Gill v. Chief Election Commissioner
Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
legal_explanation: Elections can only be challenged via an election petition before the High Court, and courts cannot interfere in the electoral process once started.

related_cases: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India
PUCL v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Voters have a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a) to know candidate antecedents, including assets, liabilities, and criminal records.

related_cases: Lily Thomas v. Union of India
Jaya Bachchan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Articles 102 and 191 lay down grounds for disqualification of MPs/MLAs, including holding an office of profit, unsound mind, bankruptcy, or criminal conviction.

related_cases: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
Shrimanth Balasaheb Patil v. Speaker, Karnataka
legal_explanation: The Tenth Schedule disqualifies legislators who voluntarily give up party membership or vote against the party whip, subject to judicial review.

related_cases: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
Keisham Meghachandra Singh v. Speaker, Manipur
legal_explanation: The decisions of the legislative Speaker under the Tenth Schedule are subject to judicial review on grounds of mala fides or violation of natural justice.

related_cases: Kuldip Nayar v. Union of India
Jyoti Basu v. Debi Ghosal
legal_explanation: The right to vote is a constitutional/statutory right under Article 326, which mandates elections based on adult suffrage without discrimination.

related_cases: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
A.D.M. Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
legal_explanation: The President can declare a National Emergency under Article 352 if the security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion.

related_cases: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
A.D.M. Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Emergency provisions enable centralization of power. Article 356 permits the dissolution of a state government in case of constitutional machinery failure.
Article 360 allows the President to declare a Financial Emergency if the financial stability or credit of India or any part thereof is threatened.

related_cases: A.D.M. Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
Makhan Singh v. State of Punjab
legal_explanation: During an emergency, Article 358 suspends Article 19, and Article 359 permits the suspension of the right to enforce other rights (excluding Articles 20 and 21).

related_cases: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab
Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
legal_explanation: The Governor exercises discretionary powers under Article 163, including appointing the CM and reserving bills for Presidential assent.

related_cases: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
Shivraj Singh Chouhan v. Speaker, MP
legal_explanation: The Governor cannot dismiss a Council of Ministers enjoying majority support without a floor test, which remains the mandatory venue for proving majority.

related_cases: Union of India v. H.S. Dhillon
State of WB v. Kesoram Industries
legal_explanation: Parliament has plenary powers to make laws for the whole of India on Union List and Concurrent List subjects, along with residuary legislative power under Article 248.

related_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
legal_explanation: The Basic Structure Doctrine limits Parliament's power under Article 368, stating that the core identity and essential features of the Constitution cannot be amended.

related_cases: A.D.M. Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
Sunil Batra v. Delhi Administration
legal_explanation: Habeas Corpus is a writ issued to secure the release of a person illegally detained, directing the detaining authority to produce the person before court.

related_cases: Gujarat State Financial Corp. v. Lotus Hotels
State of Mysore v. Chandrasekhara
legal_explanation: Mandamus is a writ directing a public authority or government official to perform a statutory duty that they have failed to execute.

related_cases: Hari Vishnu Kamath v. Ahmad Ishaque
T.C. Basappa v. T. Nagappa
legal_explanation: Certiorari is a writ issued to quash an order passed by a lower court or tribunal that exceeded its jurisdiction or committed an error of law.

related_cases: East India Commercial Co. v. Collector of Customs
legal_explanation: Prohibition is a writ issued to prevent a lower court or tribunal from continuing proceedings that exceed its jurisdictional competence.

related_cases: University of Mysore v. Govinda Rao
G.D. Karkare v. Shevde
legal_explanation: Quo Warranto is a writ issued to prevent illegal usurpation of a public office, calling upon the holder to show by what authority they hold office.

related_cases: State of West Bengal v. Union of India
State of Karnataka v. State of Andhra Pradesh
State of Karnataka v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Federal disputes between the Center and States, or between multiple States, fall under the exclusive original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 131.

related_cases: State of Karnataka v. State of AP
Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal Case
legal_explanation: Article 262 permits Parliament to provide for the adjudication of disputes relating to the use, distribution, or control of waters of inter-state rivers or river valleys, keeping it out of courts.

related_cases: In Re Delhi Laws Act Case
J.K. Industries v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Delegated legislation allows the executive to make rules to implement laws, but legislature cannot delegate its essential legislative functions.

related_cases: K.C. Gajapati Narayan Deo v. State of Orissa
State of Bihar v. Kameshwar Singh
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Colorable Legislation states that the legislature cannot do indirectly what it is prohibited from doing directly under the guise of an apparent power.

related_cases: Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce
State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Pith and Substance determines the legislative competence of a law when it encroaches on another list, by looking at its true nature and character.

related_cases: In Re Central Provinces Motor Spirit Act
Gujarat University v. Krishna
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Harmonious Construction requires conflicting provisions of a statute or different entries in legislative lists to be interpreted in a way that avoids clash and gives effect to both.

related_cases: Randhir Singh v. Union of India
State of Haryana v. Charanjit Singh
legal_explanation: Equal Pay for Equal Work is a constitutional goal under Article 39(d) read with Article 14, ensuring no discrimination in compensation for similar work.

related_cases: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Francis Coralie Mullin v. Union Territory of Delhi
legal_explanation: The right to life under Article 21 does not mean mere animal existence. It includes the right to live with human dignity and all that goes along with it.

related_cases: Municipal Corporation of Delhi v. Female Workers (Muster Roll)
State of Punjab v. Devinder Singh
legal_explanation: Maternity benefits are recognized as an essential aspect of the right to life and dignity under Article 21, mirroring the Directive Principle in Article 42.

related_cases: Santhosh Devi v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The rights of senior citizens to maintenance and medical care are derived from Article 21's right to life and Article 41's directive to assist in old age.

related_cases: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
Sheela Barse v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) relaxes the strict rule of locus standi, allowing any public-spirited citizen to approach courts to protect the rights of marginalized sections.

related_cases: D.C. Wadhwa v. State of Bihar
Krishna Kumar Singh v. State of Bihar
legal_explanation: The President (Article 123) and Governors (Article 213) can promulgate ordinances when legislature is not in session, but repromulgation without legislative review is a fraud on the Constitution.

related_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The core values of the Constitution like secularism, democracy, federalism, and judicial review form its basic structure, which cannot be destroyed by Parliament.

related_cases: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (Second Judges Case)
Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (NJAC Case)
legal_explanation: Judicial appointments to the Supreme Court and High Courts are managed by the Collegium system, emphasizing judicial independence as part of the basic structure.

related_cases: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
legal_explanation: Separation of Powers is a basic feature of the Indian Constitution, preventing any organ of the state from usurping the essential functions of the others.

related_cases: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Directive Principles are non-justiciable guidelines for state policy, aimed at establishing a welfare state and securing social and economic justice.

related_cases: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala
AIIMS Students Union v. AIIMS
legal_explanation: Article 51A prescribes eleven fundamental duties for every citizen of India, which serve as moral obligations to promote patriotism and national unity.

related_cases: Jaya Bachchan v. Union of India
Pradyut Bordoloi v. Swapan Roy
legal_explanation: A legislator is disqualified if they hold an 'office of profit' under the government, to prevent conflict of interest and maintain separation of executive and legislature.

related_cases: MSM Sharma v. Sri Krishna Sinha (Searchlight Case)
Powers, Privileges and Immunities of State Legislatures (Keshav Singh Case)
legal_explanation: Article 105 and 194 guarantee freedom of speech inside legislatures and immunity from court proceedings for statements made during legislative sessions.

related_cases: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
legal_explanation: Schedules lists details not found in the main articles, such as state territories, salaries, oath formats, tribal area administration, and anti-defection.

related_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan
legal_explanation: Article 368 outlines the procedure for amending the Constitution, requiring special majorities and, in some cases, state ratification, bound by the basic structure.

related_cases: In Re Berubari Union Case
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Preamble is the key to the minds of the makers of the Constitution, declaring India to be a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic, and part of its basic structure.

related_cases: Pradeep Jain v. Union of India
Sarbananda Sonowal v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Articles 5-11 govern citizenship in India, with Parliament having absolute power under Article 11 to regulate the acquisition and termination of citizenship.

related_cases: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab
legal_explanation: The executive power of the Union is vested in the President, exercised in accordance with the aid and advise of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.

related_cases: Kehar Singh v. Union of India
Epuru Sudhakar v. State of AP
Shatrughan Chauhan v. Union of India
legal_explanation: Article 72 grants the President power to pardon, suspend, remit, or commute sentences, subject to limited judicial review for arbitrary or discriminatory exercise.

related_cases: State of Haryana v. Jagdish
Epuru Sudhakar v. State of AP
legal_explanation: Article 161 grants the Governor power to pardon or commute sentences for offenses against state laws, subject to judicial review.

related_cases: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
Supreme Court AoR Association v. UOI
legal_explanation: The Supreme Court of India acts as the guardian of the Constitution, a court of record, and possesses original, appellate, advisory, and special leave jurisdiction.

related_cases: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
State of Karnataka v. Union of India
legal_explanation: High Courts act as the highest judicial body in states, possessing wide writ jurisdiction under Article 226 and superintendence power over subordinate courts under Article 227.

related_cases: Bengal Immunity Co. v. State of Bihar
Union of India v. Raghubir Singh
legal_explanation: Article 141 provides that the law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India, establishing the doctrine of stare decisis.

related_cases: In Re Delhi Laws Act
In Re Kerala Education Bill
In Re Ayodhya Reference
legal_explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer questions of law or public importance to the Supreme Court for its consultative opinion.

related_cases: Pritam Singh v. State
Kunhayamu v. State of Kerala
legal_explanation: Article 136 grants the Supreme Court plenary and discretionary power to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment, decree, or sentence of any court or tribunal in India.

related_cases: Bhikaji Narain Dhakras v. State of MP
Deep Chand v. State of UP
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Eclipse states that pre-constitutional laws conflicting with fundamental rights do not become void ab initio, but remain dormant (eclipsed) until constitutional amendment removes the shadow.

related_cases: R.M.D. Chamarbaugwalla v. Union of India
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Severability states that if a part of a statute is unconstitutional, only the invalid part is struck down, provided it can be separated from the valid portion.

related_cases: Basheshar Nath v. CIT
Behram Khurshid Pesikaka v. State of Bombay
legal_explanation: The Doctrine of Waiver states that a citizen cannot waive or volunteer to give up their fundamental rights under the Constitution, as they are public safeguards.

related_cases: M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath
F.N. Balsara v. State of Bombay
legal_explanation: The Public Trust Doctrine states that natural resources like rivers, forests, and seashore are held by the state in trusteeship for the public, and cannot be privatized.

related_cases: Charanjit Lal Chowdhury v. Union of India
State of Bihar v. Kameshwar Singh
legal_explanation: Eminent Domain is the inherent power of the sovereign state to acquire private property for public use upon payment of compensation.

related_cases: State of Karnataka v. Appa Balu Ingale
legal_explanation: Civil rights protection ensures that no social disabilities can be enforced against citizens based on caste or origin, backed by legislation.

related_cases: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corp.
Sudama Singh v. Government of Delhi
legal_explanation: Evictions must follow fair procedure, including notice, and alternative rehabilitation plans to protect the right to shelter and livelihood.

related_cases: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Precautionary Principle mandates that the state must take preventive action to protect the environment without waiting for absolute scientific certainty of damage.

related_cases: Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India
Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
legal_explanation: The Polluter Pays Principle makes the polluter liable to pay compensation for environmental damage and cover the cost of ecological restoration.

related_cases: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Shriram Food and Fertilizers Case)
legal_explanation: Absolute liability holds hazardous and inherently dangerous enterprises liable for any escape or damage, without allowing standard strict liability exceptions.
